title: Instagram — System Design
description: Design a photo/video sharing app: upload media, process it into multiple sizes, and serve a ranked home feed of posts from accounts you follow. The themes are the media upload/processing pipeline, CDN delivery, feed fanout (push/pull hybrid), and feed ranking — a media-heavy variant of the feed problem.

nodes.label: Mobile Client
nodes.description: Uploads photos/videos and reads a ranked home feed.

nodes.label: API Gateway / LB
nodes.description: Routes upload, feed, and interaction requests.

nodes.label: Upload Service
nodes.description: Accepts media uploads (resumable) and creates the post record.

nodes.label: Media Workers
nodes.description: Generate resized variants, thumbnails, video renditions.

nodes.label: Variant / Derivative Store
nodes.description: Processed image sizes and video renditions.

nodes.label: Post Service
nodes.description: Owns post metadata, captions, and publish state.

nodes.label: Social Graph Service
nodes.description: Follower/following edges for fanout and feed assembly.

nodes.label: Feed Fanout Service
nodes.description: Pushes new post ids into followers' feed lists.

nodes.label: Feed Service
nodes.description: Assembles, ranks, and hydrates the home feed on read.

nodes.label: Ranking Service
nodes.description: Scores feed candidates by relevance/engagement signals.

functional: Upload a photo or video with a caption.
Process media into multiple sizes/renditions for different devices.
Read a home feed of recent posts from accounts you follow, ranked.
Follow/unfollow accounts; feed reflects the follow set.
View a user's profile grid; like/comment (interactions).
nonFunctional: Read-heavy: feed reads vastly outnumber posts.
Feed read latency low (p99 < 200 ms) — precomputed, not assembled per request.
Media served fast globally via CDN; uploads processed asynchronously.
Durable media storage; posts visible within seconds of processing.
Scale to hundreds of millions of users, heavy-tailed follower counts.

capacity.label: Processing jobs
capacity.note: ~5 variants/renditions per post; CPU/GPU-heavy transcode drives the worker fleet.

capacity.label: Media storage
capacity.note: 100M posts/day × ~10MB/post (≈2MB original + variants/renditions) ≈ 1PB/day raw, ~365PB/yr before retention tiers and the replication factor (×2–3). Lifecycle: hot variants on SSD/CDN-backed tiers, cold originals on cheaper object-storage tiers.

capacity.label: Feed read QPS
capacity.note: Reads >> posts; each read drives candidate hydration + heavy CDN media egress.

capacity.label: Fanout writes
capacity.note: Posts/sec × average follower count; spikes far higher on popular accounts — bounded by the hybrid push/pull split (celebrities are pulled, not pushed).

capacity.label: Feed-cache memory
capacity.note: Cap ~500–1000 post ids/user; cold users evicted, rebuilt from pull on return.

capacity.label: Candidates per read
capacity.note: A few hundred pushed + pulled candidates per read; the ranker scores that small set and returns the top ~20 per page.

capacity.label: Feed latency
capacity.note: Precomputed candidates + hot hydration; ranker budget ~50–80 ms with a fallback.

api.description: Open a resumable upload session for a large photo/video. Returns an uploadId plus pre-signed part URLs the client PUTs media chunks to directly (bypassing the app servers). The server allocates the object-store key and tracks the session so abandoned uploads can be reaped.

Commit a finished upload: the upload service verifies all parts and the checksum, finalizes the object in the media store, and marks the session ready to publish. A failed checksum rejects the session so a corrupt original never enters the pipeline.

Publish a post from a completed upload session (metadata/commit only — no raw media flows here). Takes the uploadId from the completed session plus caption; returns fast, processing is async. An Idempotency-Key header collapses client retries so a double-tapped publish creates one post.
Follow an account; future posts fan out to this follower.
Unfollow an account; the followee's posts stop fanning out and are filtered from the feed at hydration time until the cached entries age out.
Profile grid: a user's own ready posts, newest-first, cursor-paginated. Served from an author/time index, not the home feed.
Like a post (idempotent per user/post via the like's natural key). Records an interaction event; the displayed aggregate count is updated asynchronously.
Unlike a post (idempotent). Removes the user's like row and emits a decrement event.
Add a comment. Stored per post and read back via a paginated comment list.
Read a post's comments, newest- or oldest-first, cursor-paginated; takedowns/blocked authors are filtered at read time.
Poll a post's processing state after upload (uploading, processing, ready, failed, blocked).

Delete or take down a post. Flips the post status to deleted/blocked (the authoritative source feed hydration consults), purges its media variants from the CDN, and schedules the original/variants for cleanup. Cached feed ids are not rewritten — the post is dropped at hydration and ages out of feed caches.

Block (or, with DELETE, unblock) another account. Block/mute state is consulted at feed and comment hydration to filter content in both directions; like unfollow, it is enforced at read time rather than by rewriting cached feeds.

For private accounts, follow attempts create a pending follow request; accepting it sets follows.status = accepted so future posts fan out. Private-account posts are filtered at hydration for non-followers.

dataModel.name: posts
dataModel.note: Authoritative post metadata, sharded by author.

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(uploading, processing, ready, failed, deleted, blocked)

dataModel.name: follows
dataModel.note: Directed social graph (two indexes: by follower, by followee). status carries the private-account follow-request lifecycle.

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(pending, accepted) — pending for private-account requests

dataModel.name: account_settings
dataModel.note: Per-user account state consulted at hydration to decide visibility (private accounts gate non-followers; suspended/deactivated accounts are filtered everywhere).

dataModel.name: blocks_mutes
dataModel.note: Relationship-level access control. Blocks hide content in both directions; mutes suppress content one-way. Read at feed/comment hydration so visibility changes never rewrite cached feeds.

dataModel.name: home_feed
dataModel.note: Derived per-user candidate feed in the feed cache. Entries store light metadata (not copied posts) so hydration can filter and rank without a post fetch; source/fanout_version make pushes idempotent and let pull dedupe against pushed ids.

fields.name: feed_entry
fields.type: { post_id, author_id, published_at, inserted_at, source(push|pull), fanout_version }

dataModel.name: feed_cursor_snapshot
dataModel.note: Short-lived, per-read token state for stable ranked pagination: captures the ranked candidate order so page 2 doesn't reshuffle. Expires after a few minutes; not durable feed state.

fields.name: high_watermark
fields.type: bigint (newest post id included in this snapshot)

dataModel.name: profile_posts
dataModel.note: Author/time index over ready posts; serves the profile grid (newest-first by author) without scanning home feeds.

dataModel.name: likes
dataModel.note: One row per (user, post): exact user-visible like state and dedup. Aggregate display counts are approximate and derived elsewhere.

dataModel.name: comments
dataModel.note: Comments per post, paginated by id; sharded by post_id.

dataModel.name: post_counters
dataModel.note: Approximate aggregate counts per post (likes, comments, views) for display and ranking; updated async, separate from exact per-user like rows.

technologyChoices.concern: Edge routing, upload sessions, and request admission
tradeoff: Self-hosted proxies give precise control over resumable upload routing, canarying, and request admission, but the team owns regional failover and surge capacity.
Managed gateways and load balancers remove most edge operations, but per-request cost and payload-size limits matter for media-heavy APIs.
A small Redis-backed admission limiter protects upload/session creation when media bursts exceed worker capacity.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed edge with WAF, autoscaling targets, and health checks removes most hand-built load-balancer failover and request-shedding plumbing.

technologyChoices.concern: Original and derivative media object storage
tradeoff: Object storage fits immutable originals and generated variants, but lifecycle policy and replication cost dominate at hundreds of PB per year.
Keeping originals on colder tiers and hot variants close to the CDN reduces cost while preserving reprocessing ability.
Self-hosted object storage can lower unit cost at scale, but the team owns durability, repair, geo-replication, and capacity planning.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed object lifecycle rules and CDN origin integration remove most custom blob-tiering and origin-cache invalidation code.

technologyChoices.concern: Media processing queue, workflow, and DLQ
tradeoff: A queue is simple for resize/transcode jobs, retries, and DLQs; a stream is better when multiple consumers need replayable media lifecycle events.
A workflow engine makes multi-step processing, moderation, retries, and compensation explicit, but adds operational complexity.
At-least-once delivery is acceptable only because workers write deterministic variant keys and the post state machine is idempotent.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed queues with DLQs and lag metrics remove much of the hand-built retry, poison-job, and worker-offset bookkeeping.

tradeoff: Self-hosted workers give codec and GPU/CPU control for FFmpeg-heavy video work, but autoscaling and sandboxing are on the platform team.
Serverless works well for thumbnails and short images, while long video transcodes usually need containers, batch jobs, or GPU-aware pools.
Safety scanning belongs in the same asynchronous pipeline so unsafe media never reaches the ready/published state.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed batch/serverless runtimes remove most custom worker fleet scheduling for bursty image jobs, though long video pipelines still need explicit capacity control.

technologyChoices.concern: Post metadata, profile grid, and interaction source-of-truth
tradeoff: Wide-column or KV stores fit author/time profile reads, post-id hydration, and high write volume when access patterns are explicit.
Relational stores are strong for the early post state machine and constraints, but very hot post/comment/like paths require sharding or derived indexes.
The exact like row and approximate counter are deliberately separate so user-visible state stays correct while display counts can lag.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed wide-column/KV store with conditional writes and global replication removes much of the custom post-shard placement and failover code.

technologyChoices.concern: Social graph adjacency and visibility state
tradeoff: Two adjacency indexes, follower-to-followee and followee-to-follower, make both feed reads and fanout writes efficient.
A graph database is conceptually attractive, but the hot path mostly needs sharded adjacency lists plus current privacy, block, mute, and follow status checks.
Caching celebrity follower pages helps fanout, but authoritative visibility state must still be consulted at hydration.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed wide-column/KV store with conditional writes reduces custom graph-shard failover and removes the need for a bespoke graph database on the feed hot path.

tradeoff: A partitioned log fits publish events, replay, and multiple consumers such as fanout, ranking features, and counters.
A simple queue is easier for per-follower fanout jobs and DLQs, but weaker for replaying a missed publish window.
Partitioning by author or follower shard controls lag, while idempotent writes make retries safe.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with retention, consumer lag metrics, and DLQ support removes most custom replay and offset-management code.

technologyChoices.concern: Home feed cache and stable cursor snapshots
tradeoff: In-memory lists give sub-200 ms feed reads, but home feeds are derived state and must be rebuildable from the pull path.
A durable KV/wide-column feed store survives cache loss better, but costs more and can make writes slower at fanout scale.
Short-lived cursor snapshots keep ranked pagination stable without turning the ranked feed into durable state.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed cache or KV store with replication and TTLs removes most custom feed-cache failover and cursor-expiry plumbing.

technologyChoices.concern: Ranking, feature serving, and model fallback
tradeoff: Dedicated model serving keeps feed ranking independent of feed assembly, but p99 latency must be bounded with timeouts and chronological fallback.
A feature cache improves online scoring latency, but stale engagement features can make ranking less fresh.
Managed ML endpoints speed iteration and deployment, while self-hosting gives tighter control over model runtime, batching, and cost.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed feature store and model endpoint remove much of the custom model rollout, online feature cache, and autoscaling control plane.

technologyChoices.concern: Engagement event stream and counter aggregation
tradeoff: A stream processor avoids synchronously incrementing a hot post counter row for every like or view.
Approximate display counters and ranking signals can lag, but exact per-user like rows still answer whether this viewer liked the post.
Analytical stores are useful for aggregate dashboards and model features, but should not sit on the interactive feed-read path.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream processor with windowing and state checkpoints removes much of the custom counter-shard and backfill machinery.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability for media backlog, fanout lag, and feed p99
tradeoff: The useful SLOs are path-specific: upload session success, media queue age, processing failure rate, fanout lag, feed-cache hit rate, ranker timeout rate, and feed p99.
Self-hosted telemetry gives control over retention and cardinality, but managed observability reduces pager toil and regional collector operations.
Tracing across upload, worker, fanout, feed, and ranker paths is necessary because most failures cross service boundaries.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed metrics, tracing, and dashboards remove most custom log aggregation and queue-lag alerting infrastructure.

patterns.name: Async media processing
patterns.what: Accept the upload fast; resize/transcode into variants via a queue + workers.
patterns.whenToUse: Heavy media processing that must not block the user.

patterns.name: Multi-variant + CDN delivery
patterns.what: Generate device-appropriate sizes and serve them from a CDN edge.
patterns.whenToUse: Image/video apps with global read fanout.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-write feed
patterns.what: Push a new post id into each follower's precomputed feed at publish.

patterns.name: Hybrid fanout (celebrity pull)
patterns.what: Push for normal accounts, pull celebrities at read time, merge.

patterns.name: Candidate generation + ranking
patterns.what: Gather candidate posts cheaply, then rank the smaller set by engagement signals.
patterns.whenToUse: Feeds ordered by relevance, not strict recency.

patterns.name: Reference-by-id feed
patterns.what: Store post ids in feeds; hydrate post + media URLs on read.
patterns.whenToUse: A post appears in many feeds — store once, reference by id.

patterns.name: Async engagement counting
patterns.what: Record interactions as events; aggregate dedup'd events into approximate display/ranking counters instead of incrementing a row synchronously.
patterns.whenToUse: Viral content where a single counter row would be a write hotspot, and approximate counts are good enough.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Synchronous Upload + Assemble-on-Read Feed
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. The app server accepts an upload, stores one image at its original size, and returns only after the write finishes — no async processing, no variants. Media is served straight from the app server / origin store. The home feed is assembled on read: query every account you follow for their recent posts, merge, sort by time, and return.

This is correct and trivially understandable — and it exposes the three problems the rest of the design exists to solve. First, a synchronous upload that resizes/transcodes blocks the user for seconds and can't absorb media bursts. Second, serving one original-size blob from the origin to every viewer is slow globally and melts the origin on a viral post. Third, fanning a feed read across hundreds of followees per request is far too slow at read-heavy scale.
So the baseline motivates everything that follows: process media asynchronously into device variants, serve them from a CDN, and precompute feeds with fanout instead of assembling per read.

view.caption: The mobile client uploads through the gateway to the upload service, which stores the original and creates a post, while the feed service assembles the home feed by pulling follows and hydrating posts on read.

steps.decisionPrompt: If the upload resizes inline and the feed queries every followee on read, what happens at 100M posts/day and feed reads that dwarf posts?
whyNow: Establishing the naive baseline first makes it obvious why media must be processed async and served from a CDN, and why feeds must be precomputed — every later step removes a specific pain from here.

recap.after: Sync upload of one image size, served from the origin; feed assembled by querying followees on read.
recap.newRisk: Inline processing blocks posting, origin serving doesn't scale globally, and per-read feed assembly is far too slow.

traps.trap: Shipping the synchronous-upload, assemble-on-read design as 'good enough'.
traps.why: Inline media processing blocks the user and can't burst, origin serving collapses on viral reads, and fanning every feed read across followees is infeasible at read-heavy scale.
traps.instead: Treat it as the baseline: process media async into variants, serve via CDN, and precompute feeds with fanout.

steps.title: 2. Async Media Upload and Processing
description: A post is media-first. Large media goes up via a resumable / pre-signed object-store upload first; the upload service then creates a post record in 'processing' and returns immediately — heavy work happens asynchronously via a processing queue.

Workers generate the device variants the app needs: multiple image sizes (thumbnail, feed, full) and, for video, transcoded renditions. They write variants to a derivative store and mark the post 'ready' (publishable).

Make the pipeline correct, not just fast. An Idempotency-Key on create collapses client retries to one post, and the post moves through an explicit state machine (uploading → processing → ready, plus failed / blocked / deleted). Workers process jobs idempotently (a retried job overwrites the same variant keys), validate and re-encode media, strip EXIF, and run moderation. A job that exhausts its retries lands in a dead-letter queue and the post is marked failed — surfaced via GET /posts/{id}/status — rather than stuck in processing forever.
Decoupling upload from processing keeps posting fast and lets the processing fleet scale with media volume independently.

view.caption: The upload service stores the original and enqueues a processing job; media workers pull it, read the original, write variants, and tell the post service the post is ready.

options.name: Async queue + worker fleet (default)
options.description: Upload stores the original and enqueues a job; a worker fleet generates image and video variants asynchronously, then marks the post ready.
pros: Upload returns as soon as the original is durably stored, so posting stays fast regardless of media size.
The processing fleet scales with media volume independently of the request path.
A burst of uploads buffers in the queue instead of overloading the upload tier.
cons: The post is briefly in 'processing' and not yet publishable — eventual readiness, not instant.
More moving parts: a queue, workers, and a state machine to mark posts ready.
A worker backlog delays publish; needs autoscaling and queue monitoring.

view.caption: The upload service enqueues a job on the processing queue that media workers consume to generate variants and then publish the post, keeping the request fast.

options.name: Synchronous processing in the upload request
options.description: The upload request itself resizes images and transcodes video inline, returning only once every variant is generated and the post is ready.
pros: The post is fully ready the moment upload returns — no 'processing' state to manage.
No queue or worker fleet to operate; the simplest possible pipeline.
Trivial to reason about: one request does everything end to end.
cons: The user waits seconds while images resize and video transcodes — bad for large media.
Upload servers must carry CPU-heavy transcoding, coupling request capacity to media work.
A burst of uploads (or one large video) starves the request tier and times out.

view.caption: The upload service resizes and transcodes the media inline before creating the post, so all processing happens within the upload request.

options.name: Serverless functions per media object
options.description: An object-storage upload event triggers a serverless function per file that produces its variants, with no standing worker fleet.
pros: No standing worker fleet to size or pay for between bursts — the upload event itself triggers the work.
Scales to zero when idle and fans out massively for a spike, paying only for work actually done.
Each variant/rendition can be its own short function, isolating failures.
cons: Cold starts and per-invocation limits make long video transcodes awkward or impossible.
Less control over batching, GPU access, and large media memory than a dedicated fleet.
Per-invocation cost can exceed a busy worker fleet at sustained high volume.

view.caption: Storing the original in the object store fires an object-created trigger to serverless media functions, which write variants and mark the post ready.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why process media asynchronously instead of resizing during the upload request?

concepts.definition: Store the original fast; resize/transcode into variants via a queue + workers.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps posting responsive and absorbs media-processing bursts.
concepts.example: Upload -> store original -> enqueue -> 202; workers create variants seconds later.

whyNow: Media processing is the upload-side heart of Instagram; the async pipeline is what makes posting fast and is the precondition for serving and feeds.

recap.after: Originals stored, variants generated async, post becomes ready.
recap.newRisk: Variants must be delivered to millions of viewers globally and fast.

traps.trap: Treating async upload as just 'queue + workers' with no idempotency or failure path.
traps.why: Client retries can create duplicate posts, worker retries can publish twice, and a failed transcode leaves the post stuck in 'processing' forever.
traps.instead: Use an Idempotency-Key on create, idempotent jobs keyed to fixed variant paths, an explicit state machine (uploading → processing → ready / failed / blocked), and a DLQ that marks the post failed.

traps.trap: Accepting media without validation or moderation.
traps.why: Poison/oversized media can crash workers, and unsafe content reaches viewers; EXIF can leak location.
traps.instead: Validate and re-encode media, strip EXIF, and gate publish on a moderation check (blocked is a terminal state).

flows.name: Async upload → process → publish
flows.note: Upload returns fast after storing the original; workers create variants off the hot path and mark the post ready.

steps.title: 3. Variants and CDN Delivery
description: Different devices and contexts need different media: a small thumbnail in the grid, a feed-sized image, full resolution on tap; adaptive video renditions. Generating variants once at upload avoids on-the-fly resizing on every read.

Serve all variants from a CDN, not the origin store. The feed returns variant URLs; clients fetch the right size from the nearest edge, absorbing the enormous read fanout that media-heavy feeds create.
Post metadata (small, transactional) stays in the post store; media (large) lives in object storage behind the CDN — the metadata/blob/CDN split.

view.caption: The mobile client fetches pre-generated variants from the media CDN, which pulls from the variant store on a cache miss while the feed service hydrates posts.

options.name: Pre-generate variants at upload, serve via CDN (default)
options.description: All image sizes and video renditions are produced once at upload and stored as static objects the CDN caches and serves from the edge.
pros: Resizing/transcoding happens once at upload, so reads never pay generation cost.
Every variant is a static object the CDN can cache and serve from the edge.
A viral post is served entirely from cached variants — the origin and workers aren't touched per view.
cons: Storage grows with every size/rendition multiplied by every post (storage amplification).
Generates variants that may never be requested for unpopular posts.
Adding a new size later means a backfill job over existing media.

view.caption: Variants are pre-generated at upload, so the client reads them from the media CDN and the CDN only origin-fetches the variant store on a miss.

options.name: On-the-fly resize at an image service (CDN-cached)
options.description: Only the original is stored; an image service derives the requested size on first request and the CDN caches that variant thereafter.
pros: Store only the original; derive any size on first request, so no upfront variant explosion.
Adding a new device size needs no backfill — just a new transform parameter.
Storage stays minimal; you only ever materialize sizes that are actually requested.
cons: First request for each size pays a resize latency (cold-miss) before the CDN caches it.
A resize service must be scaled and protected against abusive parameter combinations.
Less predictable read latency than serving fully pre-baked objects.

view.caption: On a CDN miss the request hits an on-the-fly resize service that reads the original from the media object store and returns a resized image to cache.

steps.decisionPrompt: A viral post is viewed by 50M people. What serves the image, and what doesn't get touched?

concepts.definition: Resize once at upload; serve device-appropriate sizes from CDN edge.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids per-read resizing and keeps the origin/store off the read path.
concepts.example: Grid uses 150px thumb; feed uses 1080px; full on tap — all CDN-cached.

whyNow: CDN delivery of pre-made variants is how media reads scale; it's the read-path payoff of the upload pipeline.

recap.after: Right-sized media served globally from CDN.
recap.newRisk: Reads also need the feed itself — which posts, in what order, assembled fast.

steps.title: 4. Feed Generation: Hybrid Fanout
description: Assembling a feed by querying every followee on each read is too slow. Precompute it: when a post is published (ready), fan out its id into each follower's candidate feed list (fanout-on-write) so reads are a cheap cached lookup.

Celebrities break pure push (one post = millions of feed writes), so use a hybrid: push for normal accounts, and pull the few celebrities a user follows at read time, merging both. This is the same push/pull tradeoff as a text feed, applied to media posts.
Store only post ids in feeds; the post and its media URLs are hydrated on read (a post lives once, referenced by many feeds).

Operationally: bound the read-time pull (fetch at most M pull-mode authors × K recent posts each, dedup by post_id), partition the fanout queue by follower shard and batch writes, and make feed-cache inserts idempotent (a retried fanout writes the same post id once). When an account crosses the push/pull threshold, use a classification version or cutoff timestamp so posts aren't double-counted or dropped during the flip. Surface per-author fanout lag as a metric and alert on it.

view.caption: On publish the post service notifies the fanout service, which gets followers from the social graph and pushes the post id into feed caches; the feed service reads those plus pulls celebrity follows.

options.name: Hybrid fanout (push normal, pull celebrities) (default)
options.description: Normal accounts' posts are pushed into each follower's feed list, while a few followed celebrities' posts are pulled and merged at read time.
pros: Reads are a cheap cached lookup for the bulk of accounts (push), keeping feed reads fast.
Celebrities are pulled at read time, so one post never triggers tens of millions of feed writes.
A single threshold knob trades write amplification against read-merge cost.
cons: The read path must merge two sources (cached push list + pulled celebrity posts) and dedup.
Classifying accounts as push vs pull adds logic and edge cases around the threshold.
More complex than either pure strategy to implement and operate.

view.caption: The fanout service pushes each normal post into followers' feed caches, while the feed service pulls celebrity posts from the social graph at read time.

options.name: Pure fanout-on-write (push to every follower)
options.description: When a post is published, its id is written into every follower's precomputed feed list, so a read is a single list lookup.
pros: Feed reads are the cheapest possible: read one precomputed list, no merge.
Ranking and pagination operate on a single materialized candidate set.
Simple read path — all the work is done at write time.
cons: A celebrity post explodes into tens of millions of feed writes — a write storm and hotspot.
Wastes writes on inactive followers who may never open the app.
Fanout latency for huge audiences delays when the post appears in feeds.

view.caption: On every publish the fanout service fetches all followers and pushes the post id into each follower's feed cache, with no read-time pull.

options.name: Pure fanout-on-read (assemble at read time)
options.description: Nothing is precomputed; each feed read queries the user's followees, fetches their recent posts, and merges them on the fly.
pros: No write amplification: publishing a post is a single write regardless of follower count.
No precomputed feed storage to maintain or rebuild.
Always reflects the very latest posts since nothing is precomputed and stale.
cons: Each feed read queries every followee and merges — too slow at read-heavy media scale.
Read cost scales with how many accounts a user follows, not with how often they post.
Caching helps little because every user's merge is different.

view.caption: Nothing is precomputed: the feed service pulls a user's follows from the social graph and hydrates their posts entirely at read time.

steps.decisionPrompt: A celebrity with 50M followers posts. Do you push that post id into 50M feeds?

concepts.definition: Push posts from normal accounts into follower feeds; pull celebrity posts at read time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cheap reads for the common case without celebrity fanout storms.
concepts.example: Push for <1M-follower accounts; pull above the threshold and merge.

whyNow: Feed generation is the core scaling problem; the hybrid push/pull design is exactly what an interviewer probes for in any feed case.

recap.after: Precomputed candidate feeds + celebrity pull merge.
recap.newRisk: Recency alone isn't the best order — feeds are ranked by relevance.

traps.trap: Storing full posts/media in every follower's feed.
traps.why: A viral post copied into millions of feeds wastes storage and makes edits/deletes painful.
traps.instead: Store post ids; hydrate post + media URLs on read.

flows.name: Publish → hybrid fanout
flows.note: On publish, push the post id into normal followers' feeds; celebrities are left to be pulled at read time.

messages.label: push post id into each follower feed
no push — pulled at read time

steps.title: 5. Feed Ranking
description: Modern feeds aren't reverse-chronological; they're ranked by predicted engagement. The feed service treats the precomputed list (plus pulled celebrity posts) as candidates and the ranking service scores them.

This is candidate-generation + ranking: cheap recall (the fanout candidate set) then a richer scoring pass over the small set using signals (recency, affinity to the author, predicted likes/comments, media type).
Hydrate the top-N: fetch post metadata and media variant URLs, paginate by cursor. Ranking runs on the small candidate set, so it stays within the latency budget.

Ranking is now a read-path dependency, so it needs a hard timeout and a fallback: if the ranker or feature fetch exceeds ~50–80 ms, return reverse-chronological (or cached-score) candidates and alert on the timeout rate. For stable pagination, the first page mints a short-lived cursor token capturing the ranked candidate snapshot (rank position, score version, and a high-watermark) so page 2 doesn't reshuffle under the user.

view.caption: The feed service reads candidate post ids from the feed cache, sends them to the ranking service for ordering, then hydrates the ranked posts from the post store.

options.name: ML ranking over fanout candidates (default)
options.description: The fanout candidates plus pulled celebrity posts are scored by a model on engagement signals, and the top-ranked posts are returned.
pros: Scores the small candidate set by predicted engagement (affinity, predicted likes/comments, media type), surfacing the best posts.
Two-stage recall-then-rank keeps cost bounded: cheap fanout recall, rich scoring on a few hundred posts.
Engagement signals from interactions feed back, so the feed adapts to what resonates.
cons: Needs a model, feature pipeline, and training loop — significant ML infrastructure.
Ranking can feel opaque and may bury recent posts users expect to see.
A model-serving call adds latency and a dependency on the read path.

view.caption: The feed service passes fanout candidates from the feed cache to an ML ranking service that scores and orders them before posts are hydrated.

options.name: Reverse-chronological feed
options.description: The candidate set is simply sorted newest-first with no scoring pass, showing the latest posts from followed accounts in time order.
pros: Dead simple: sort the candidate set by time, no model or feature store.
Predictable and transparent — users see exactly the latest posts from who they follow.
Lowest read latency: no scoring pass on the path.
cons: Mixes high- and low-value posts equally; engagement is typically lower than ranked feeds.
A burst of posts from one chatty account can crowd out everyone else.
Leaves engagement signals unused — no adaptation to what a user actually likes.

view.caption: The feed service returns candidates from the feed cache in reverse-chronological order with no ranking step, then hydrates the posts.

options.name: Lightweight heuristic scoring
options.description: Candidates are ordered by a hand-tuned formula combining recency decay, author affinity, and post popularity, computed inline without a model.
pros: No ML stack: a hand-tuned formula (recency decay × author affinity × post popularity) is all it takes.
Cheap to compute inline, so no separate model-serving hop on the read path.
Captures most of the lift of ranking with a fraction of the complexity.
cons: Hand-tuned weights drift out of date and need manual retuning.
Can't capture nonlinear signal interactions a learned model would.
Tends to plateau below a well-trained ranking model on engagement.

view.caption: The feed service scores feed-cache candidates with a lightweight recency-times-affinity heuristic instead of a full ML model, then hydrates the posts.

steps.decisionPrompt: You have 500 candidate posts for a feed. How do you order them well without scoring everything expensively?
whyNow: Ranking is what distinguishes a modern media feed from a chronological one; the candidate-gen + rank split keeps it fast.

concepts.definition: Cheap recall of candidates, then a richer scoring pass over the small set.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the fanout simple while allowing rich, evolving ranking within budget.
concepts.example: Fanout gives ~hundreds of candidates; the ranker scores and returns the top 20.

deepDives.title: Where ranking features come from (and how models roll out)
points: Interaction events (the like/comment stream from Step 6) update nearline counters and per-(viewer, author) affinity features within seconds, so a post that's taking off is scored on near-current signal — not yesterday's snapshot.
Slow-changing features (author quality, content embeddings) are precomputed offline and read from a feature store at scoring time.

Offline jobs train models on logged impressions + interactions; new versions roll out behind a version flag and are shadow/A-B evaluated before taking traffic, so a bad model rolls back without a deploy.
The feed service pins a score_version per request (and in the cursor snapshot) so a mid-session rollout doesn't reshuffle a user's open feed.

Freshness has a floor, not a guarantee: if the feature fetch or model call blows the ~50–80 ms budget, the read path falls back to last cached scores (or chronological) rather than blocking — ranking degrades to slightly-stale ordering instead of failing the feed.

recap.newRisk: Viral posts and celebrity reads create hot spots; feeds must be rebuildable.

failureDrills.scenario: The ranker (or feature fetch) is slow or unavailable.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: After a ~50–80 ms timeout the feed falls back to reverse-chronological (or last cached scores) candidates, so the user still gets a feed.
failureDrills.mitigation: Hard read-path timeout with chronological fallback; alert on timeout rate and degrade rather than block.

failureDrills.scenario: A user paginates while underlying candidates change.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Page 2 continues from the page-1 ranking without reshuffling already-seen posts.
failureDrills.mitigation: Mint a short-lived cursor token holding the ranked snapshot (rank position, score version, high-watermark).

flows.name: Feed read: candidates → rank → hydrate
flows.note: Read merges pushed candidates with pulled celebrity posts, ranks the small set, then hydrates the top-N with media URLs.

steps.title: 6. Likes, Comments, and Counters
description: Interactions (likes, comments, views) are high-volume and feed back into ranking. A like is a write that updates an engagement signal; on viral posts a synchronous counter increment becomes a hotspot.

Separate exact user-visible state from approximate aggregates. A like is one row per (user, post) — that gives idempotent 'did I like this?' state and dedups repeat taps. The displayed *count* is approximate, aggregated asynchronously from interaction events into a per-post counter used for display and ranking, so a viral post's 1M likes/min never hammer a single row.
Comments are stored per post and read back with cursor pagination; they carry moderation state (visible / deleted / blocked) and are filtered at read time. Counts are aggregated the same async way.
These engagement signals flow back to the ranking service, so the feed reflects what's resonating — closing the loop between interaction and ranking.

view.caption: A like or comment arrives through the gateway at the post service, which persists it to the post store and feeds engagement into ranking.

options.name: Async event aggregation + approximate counters (default)
options.description: Likes and views are recorded as events on a queue and aggregated asynchronously into approximate display and ranking counters.
pros: Likes are recorded as events and aggregated, so a viral post's 1M likes/min never hammer a single counter row.
Display/ranking counts can be approximate, which is cheap and good enough for a feed.
Decouples interaction writes from the read path; aggregation scales independently.
cons: Counts are eventually consistent — a user may not see their like reflected instantly.
Needs an event pipeline and aggregation jobs (dedup per user where required).
Reconciling approximate vs exact counts adds bookkeeping.

view.caption: The post service emits each like/comment onto an interaction event stream that a counter aggregator rolls up into approximate counts in the post store.

options.name: Synchronous counter row per interaction
options.description: Each like atomically increments a single counter row on the post synchronously, so the displayed count is always exact.
pros: Counts are exact and immediately consistent the moment the like returns.
Trivial to implement: one row, one atomic increment.
No event pipeline or aggregation to operate.
cons: A viral post's single counter row becomes a write hotspot — lock contention melts it.
Couples interaction throughput directly to the database's per-row write capacity.
Synchronous increments add latency to the interaction request.

view.caption: The post service runs an atomic increment of the counter row in the post store for every interaction, updating the exact count synchronously.

options.name: Sharded / probabilistic counters
options.description: A hot post's count is spread across many sub-counters (or a sketch); writes hit a random shard and reads sum the shards on demand.
pros: Spread a hot post's count across many sub-counters (or a sketch), removing the single-row hotspot while staying near-exact.
Reads sum the shards on demand; writes pick a random shard with no contention.
Avoids a full event pipeline for the common case while handling viral keys.
cons: Reads must fan out and sum N shards (or read a sketch), adding read cost.
Choosing shard count per post is a tuning problem; over-sharding wastes space.
Probabilistic variants trade a small count error for memory savings.

view.caption: The post service increments one of N random sub-counters in a sharded counter that persists its shards to the post store, spreading hot-key write load.

steps.decisionPrompt: A post gets 1M likes/minute. Why not increment a counter row per like?
whyNow: Interactions are both a core feature and the ranking fuel; handling counters asynchronously is the expected scaling answer.

concepts.definition: Aggregate dedup'd interaction events into approximate counters instead of incrementing synchronously.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids a write hotspot on viral content and feeds ranking signals.
concepts.example: Like events -> aggregate -> approximate like count + ranking signal.

recap.newRisk: Hot shards for viral content and feed-cache loss need handling.

steps.title: 7. Scaling, Hot Content, and Rebuildable Feeds
description: Shard posts and the social graph by id; replicate the feed cache and CDN for read volume. Viral posts get extreme reads — already absorbed by CDN (media) and the post/feed caches (metadata).

Treat the feed cache as rebuildable derived state: if a user's feed shard is lost, rebuild it from the pull path (recent posts from their followees) and resume push. Fanout bursts (a popular account posts) buffer in the queue with batched workers.
Cap feed length (keep the recent window), evict cold users, and bound celebrity-pull cost with the threshold knob from the fanout step.

Filter at hydration, not at write. A post id can sit in millions of feed caches, so deletes, takedowns/moderation, private-account changes, blocks/mutes, and unfollows are all enforced when the feed service hydrates ids. It consults the authoritative state — post status (deleted/blocked), account_settings (private/suspended), blocks_mutes, and follows.status — drops ids no longer visible to this viewer, and purges the CDN on media deletion. That keeps feed entries cheap (light id metadata) and correct at once, and avoids rewriting millions of cached lists on every visibility change.

Operate it: monitor per-author fanout lag and processing-queue backlog, replay failed fanout from the DLQ, budget feed-cache rebuilds, and degrade gracefully per region (serve stale/chronological) when the ranker or graph service is slow.

view.caption: The feed service serves precomputed candidates from the feed cache that the fanout service keeps populated, pulling celebrity follows from the social graph and hydrating from the post store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A user's feed-cache shard is lost. What do they see, and how is it rebuilt?
whyNow: Closing on hot content + rebuildable feeds shows the design degrades gracefully and is operable at scale.

recap.after: Sharded, replicated, rebuildable feeds tolerant of hot content.
recap.newRisk: Fanout lag on viral posts and ranking cost — bounded by batching, caching, and the candidate cap.

bottlenecks.mitigation: CDN for media + post/feed caches for metadata; replicate shards.
Rebuild from followees' recent posts (pull) and resume push.

bottlenecks.issue: Fanout lag on a popular account's post.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Partition fanout by follower shard, batch idempotent writes, and replay from the DLQ; alert on per-author lag.

failureDrills.scenario: The feed cache is evicted for a returning user.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Feed is reassembled from followees (+ celebrity pull) so the user still sees content.
failureDrills.mitigation: Treat the feed cache as rebuildable derived state.

failureDrills.scenario: A post is deleted, taken down, or its author goes private / is unfollowed after fanout.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The post stops appearing for viewers who shouldn't see it on the next read, without rewriting millions of cached feeds.
failureDrills.mitigation: Filter post ids at hydration against current visibility/follow/block state and purge the CDN on media deletion.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Media Feed
finalDesign.description: Uploads go to durable object storage (resumable/pre-signed), then create a 'processing' post that moves through an explicit state machine; workers generate device variants (and video renditions) served via CDN, with idempotent jobs and a DLQ for failures. On publish, a hybrid fanout pushes the post id into normal followers' precomputed feeds (partitioned, idempotent) and leaves celebrities to be pulled at read time. The feed service merges pushed + pulled candidates, the ranker scores them by engagement signals (with a chronological fallback on timeout and stable cursor pagination), and the top-N are hydrated with post metadata + CDN media URLs — filtering deleted/private/blocked/unfollowed ids at hydration. Likes are exact per-user rows while display counts are aggregated asynchronously (the interaction event stream + aggregator from Step 6 is folded into the post service here); comments are stored and paginated per post. Profile grids are served from an author/time index. Feeds are sharded, replicated, and rebuildable from the pull path.

view.caption: The mobile client uploads via the gateway to the upload service, which stores originals and queues workers to write variants served by the CDN; posts fan out through the fanout service into feed caches, and the feed service ranks and hydrates the home feed. Kept compact: the Step 6 interaction event stream + counter aggregator and the profile/author index live inside the post-service/post-store boundary rather than as separate nodes.

functional.how: Social graph changes fanout targets; private accounts use pending follow requests (follows.status); unfollow stops pushes and is filtered at hydration until cached entries age out.
Author/time index over ready posts (profile_posts), hydrated with CDN media URLs — independent of the home feed.
Exact per-user like rows for 'I liked this' + dedup; async-aggregated approximate counts for display/ranking; comments stored per post and paginated.

nonFunctional.how: Sharded stores; feed cache rebuildable via pull.
Private accounts, blocks/mutes, deletes/takedowns enforced at hydration from account_settings/blocks_mutes/follows.status/post status; CDN purge on media deletion — no cached-feed rewrites.

say: Confirm media-heavy + read-heavy: process media, serve a ranked feed.
Pin fast feed reads and global fast media as the goals.
Flag the heavy-tailed follower distribution (celebrity problem).
Candidate generation + ranking for the feed.
Tradeoffs: push vs pull threshold, variant count vs storage, ranking cost vs latency.
Mention Stories/ephemeral and explore/discovery as extensions.

expectations: May miss celebrity hybrid and ranking.
Reasons about push/pull threshold and ranking cost quantitatively.
Treats feeds as rebuildable derived state.
Discusses hot-content mitigation and variant strategy precisely.

followUps: How would you add Stories (ephemeral, 24h) on top of this?
How do you build an Explore/discovery feed (content from non-followed accounts)?
How do you handle video the same pipeline does photos (transcode, adaptive)?
How do you keep feeds bounded in memory at scale?
How would you incorporate real-time engagement into ranking quickly?
How do private accounts, blocks/mutes, and post takedowns get enforced across already-fanned-out feeds?

links.groupDescription: Production case studies and papers for Instagram-like media feeds, social graphs, sharding, and geo-distributed state.
links.title: What Powers Instagram: Hundreds of Instances, Dozens of Technologies
links.why: Early production stack reference for Instagram's services, S3/CloudFront media path, Redis-backed feeds, task queues, and monitoring.

links.groupDescription: Production case studies and papers for Instagram-like media feeds, social graphs, sharding, and geo-distributed state.
links.title: Sharding & IDs at Instagram
links.why: Shows how Instagram encoded time and logical shard information into IDs to keep writes scalable while preserving feed-order semantics.

links.groupDescription: Production case studies and papers for Instagram-like media feeds, social graphs, sharding, and geo-distributed state.

links.groupDescription: Production case studies and papers for Instagram-like media feeds, social graphs, sharding, and geo-distributed state.

links.groupDescription: Upload protocols, object storage, photo/video processing, cache hierarchies, CDN behavior, and media delivery tradeoffs.
